title: useColorScheme hook that combines OS preference with user override and persists choice
scenario_context: App theme honors OS prefers-color-scheme by default. User can override (light/dark/system) via settings. Choice persists via localStorage. Reactive to OS changes when on 'system'.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Flash of wrong theme on load (FOUC)
Theme stored without listening to OS changes
responsive_reasoning: 1. Three states: 'system', 'light', 'dark'. System = follow OS. Others = force.\n2. localStorage persists choice.\n3. matchMedia listens to OS for 'system' mode.

detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Inline script before React to prevent FOUC

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Class on root element + CSS variables

performance_notes: Inline script runs before React hydrates — no FOUC. Single matchMedia listener.
accessibility_across_viewports: Respects user's OS-level preference by default. Override available for those who need it.
edge_cases_handled: First-time visitor — follows OS
User toggles OS theme — system mode follows live
User sets explicit theme — persists across reloads
User clears localStorage — falls back to system
what_breaks_naive_approach: Reading localStorage in React render causes hydration mismatch. Inline script before React fixes this. Ignoring matchMedia changes means 'system' mode doesn't update when OS changes.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Three-state model (system/light/dark). 2) Live OS following. 3) FOUC-free via inline script.

title: Comment thread that switches between flat list and nested tree based on width
scenario_context: Reddit-style comments. Mobile: too narrow for deep indentation, show flat list with replies. Desktop: indented nested threads.
responsive_reasoning: 1. Indentation eats horizontal space — fine on desktop, breaks on phone.\n2. Container width drives nesting depth.\n3. Mobile shows reply context inline.
performance_notes: CSS variable for indent. Depth cap prevents runaway.
accessibility_across_viewports: ul/li hierarchy preserved. Border-left provides visual thread indicator even when indentation is minimal.
edge_cases_handled: Deeply nested on mobile — depth capped, doesn't overflow
Wide desktop — visible indentation up to 3 levels
Single comment — no indentation needed
what_breaks_naive_approach: Fixed deep indentation on phone = unreadable. No indentation on desktop = loses structure.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Container-driven indent. 2) Depth cap. 3) Border-left provides structure even when collapsed.

title: Code blocks that scroll horizontally without breaking flow, with line numbers respecting fluid type
viewport_spectrum: Any viewport with long code lines
scenario_context: Code blocks in technical docs. Code lines often exceed mobile viewport width. Horizontal scroll within the block, line numbers stay aligned, font size fluid.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Line numbers that don't align with wrapped lines
responsive_reasoning: 1. Code requires monospace + horizontal scroll for long lines.\n2. Font size fluid but with floor (12px minimum).\n3. Line numbers in sticky column.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Horizontal scroll with sticky line numbers

interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Tab navigation works in code blocks

performance_notes: Pure CSS layout. No syntax highlighting library (Prism/Shiki for production). Sticky line number column.
accessibility_across_viewports: Real <pre> element. Copy button accessible. Line numbers aria-hidden (visual only).
edge_cases_handled: Very long lines — scroll horizontally, line numbers stay visible
Mobile narrow — font size hits floor at 12px (legible)
Many lines — block scrolls internally if max-height set (production)
RTL — pre direction preserved (code stays LTR)
what_breaks_naive_approach: Wrapping code lines breaks logic visibility. Tiny mobile font is unreadable. No sticky line numbers means scrolling right loses context.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Code stays formatted. 2) Sticky line numbers. 3) Fluid font with legible floor.

title: Grid layout with gap that scales with item size, not viewport
scenario_context: A grid of varied-size items. Gap should be proportional to item size — wider items get larger gaps, narrower items get tighter gaps. Maintains visual rhythm.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Fixed gap regardless of item size
responsive_reasoning: 1. Gap-to-item ratio matters visually.\n2. Container queries on grid drive gap.\n3. Auto-fit grids combined with container-aware gap.
performance_notes: Pure CSS. Combined auto-fit + container query gap.
accessibility_across_viewports: DOM order preserved.
edge_cases_handled: Narrow container — 1 column, tight gap
Wide container — many columns, generous gap
Single item — full width, irrelevant gap
what_breaks_naive_approach: Tight gap on wide layouts looks cramped; loose gap on mobile wastes space.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Gap scales with container. 2) Auto-fit columns. 3) Visual rhythm consistent.

title: Right-click menu replaced with long-press on touch, with consistent menu UI
scenario_context: File browser. Right-click on desktop opens context menu. On touch, long-press opens same menu. Same menu items, different trigger.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: No alternative to right-click on touch
responsive_reasoning: 1. Context menu is a function; trigger varies by input.\n2. Long-press needs ~500ms threshold to avoid conflict with scroll.\n3. PointerEvent unifies both.

detection_strategy.code_pattern: if (e.pointerType === 'touch') { setTimeout(() => showMenu(), 500); } else if (e.button === 2) { showMenu(); }

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Unified menu trigger via pointer events

interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Shift+F10 or context menu key → menu

performance_notes: Pointer events. Single timer per pointer. Menu only renders when open.
accessibility_across_viewports: Right-click + long-press + context-menu key all work. Menu has role=menu/menuitem. Touch user gets haptic feedback.
edge_cases_handled: User scrolls during long-press — pointercancel fires, timer cleared
Mouse user right-clicks — preventDefault stops browser menu
Touch user lifts before 500ms — no menu
Keyboard user (Shift+F10) — Production add: keyboard handler
what_breaks_naive_approach: Right-click only locks out touch users. Long-press without delay conflicts with scroll. Different menus drift in maintenance.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Pointer event unification. 2) Long-press timing avoids gesture conflict. 3) Haptic on touch activation.

title: Search bar that focuses and opens keyboard, but blurs when scrolling results to dismiss keyboard
scenario_context: Search bar at top of list. Tap search → keyboard appears, type query → results filter. Scroll results → blur input → keyboard dismisses, more results visible.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Keyboard staying open during scroll (eats results)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Keyboard active = limited results space. Scroll = user wants to see more.\n2. Blur on scroll start (touch) dismisses keyboard.\n3. Native UX from iOS Mail and similar apps.

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap search → type → scroll → keyboard dismisses → tap search again to type more

performance_notes: Scroll listener fires often but blur check is cheap. Native search input.
accessibility_across_viewports: Real search input. enterKeyHint='search' shows search action on virtual keyboard. Keyboard always dismissible via blur.
edge_cases_handled: User scrolls minimal amount — keyboard dismisses, can tap to re-focus
User types fast then scrolls — typing pauses, keyboard dismisses
Desktop — no blur on scroll (irrelevant)
Keyboard user — Tab away to dismiss (no auto-blur on Tab needed)
what_breaks_naive_approach: Keyboard staying open during scroll eats 50% of results visibility. Auto-focus on every render causes keyboard to flash open repeatedly.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Touch-only auto-blur (mouse doesn't trigger). 2) Maximizes results space on intent. 3) Re-focus available via tap.

title: PWA standalone mode detection with adapted UI (no browser chrome)
viewport_spectrum: PWA installed to home screen, opened in browser tab
scenario_context: Same web app behaves differently when installed as PWA (full screen, no URL bar) vs in browser tab. PWA needs: app-style nav, safe-area awareness for status bar, no back-to-browser button.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Same chrome in PWA and browser
Missing safe areas in PWA (no status bar inset)
responsive_reasoning: 1. PWA standalone = full screen — must respect safe areas.\n2. matchMedia('(display-mode: standalone)') detects PWA.\n3. Different nav patterns appropriate.

layout_strategy.information_density_changes: PWA may show more vertical space (no URL bar)

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: PWA: bottom nav. Browser tab: same plus browser back button.

performance_notes: Single matchMedia check.
accessibility_across_viewports: PWA back button explicit for users without browser chrome. Safe areas respected.
edge_cases_handled: PWA on iOS — full screen with notch
PWA on Android — gesture pill
Browser tab — standard chrome
Twinkle: PWA installed mid-session (display-mode listener fires)
what_breaks_naive_approach: No back button in PWA = users stuck without browser back. No safe area means status bar overlap.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Display mode-aware UI. 2) Safe area in standalone. 3) Explicit nav in PWA mode.

title: Image gallery lightbox that adapts crop and controls to viewport orientation
viewport_spectrum: Portrait phone (vertical image fit), landscape phone (controls on side), tablet/desktop
scenario_context: Lightbox image viewer. Portrait phone: image fills width, controls top/bottom. Landscape phone: image centered with letterbox, controls on right side.
responsive_reasoning: 1. Orientation determines image fit and control placement.\n2. Portrait: fill width. Landscape: fit height.\n3. Controls positioned to not obscure image.

layout_strategy.content_priority: Image always full visible; controls accessible

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Swipe horizontally for next/prev, tap to toggle controls, double-tap to zoom

performance_notes: Image object-contain fits any aspect. Controls positioned via CSS only.
accessibility_across_viewports: role=dialog aria-modal aria-label. Keyboard nav for prev/next/close. Buttons have aria-labels and 44pt min.
edge_cases_handled: Portrait image in landscape viewport — letterboxed
Landscape image in portrait viewport — letterboxed (controls below)
Tiny image — object-contain prevents stretch
Rotation during view — controls reposition
what_breaks_naive_approach: Bottom-only controls in landscape phone eat vertical space and crowd home indicator. Side controls in portrait obscure image edges.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Orientation-aware control placement. 2) Safe area on all sides. 3) Image fit preserved across orientations.

title: Apple Pencil-aware UI for iPad — show drawing tools when Apple Pencil detected
viewport_spectrum: iPad with/without Apple Pencil, Surface Pro with pen
scenario_context: Note-taking app shows additional drawing tools only when pen input is detected. iPad with finger only = basic UI; iPad with Apple Pencil = drawing tools appear.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Always showing drawing tools (clutters non-pen users)
responsive_reasoning: 1. PointerEvent.pointerType === 'pen' reveals pen presence at event time.\n2. Once pen detected, show drawing tools.\n3. Persist preference for session.

interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Type; draw via mouse if intentional
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Type only; keyboard shortcuts for formatting

performance_notes: Single pointer listener. Drawing canvas (not shown) only mounts when pen detected.
accessibility_across_viewports: Drawing is enhancement. Text editing always primary. Tools have aria-labels.
edge_cases_handled: User taps with finger — no pen detected, basic UI
User uses Apple Pencil — pen detected, tools appear
Multi-input session — first pen event reveals tools, stays revealed
Pen not present on device — tools never appear
what_breaks_naive_approach: Always-showing tools clutters UI for finger-only users. Never-showing locks out pen capability.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Capability detection on first event. 2) Progressive enhancement. 3) Pen events distinguish from touch.

title: VS Code-style activity bar + side panel + editor + terminal that all collapse responsively
viewport_spectrum: Mobile (single pane), tablet (editor + activity bar), desktop (all four)
scenario_context: Code editor IDE web app. Desktop: activity bar (icons) + side panel (file tree) + editor + terminal at bottom. Mobile: just editor, panels accessible via slide-out drawers.
responsive_reasoning: 1. Each pane has same data; presentation changes.\n2. Mobile shows editor primarily; others on demand.\n3. Activity bar always accessible as button on mobile.

layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Mobile single pane; desktop quadrant layout

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap activity icon → side panel drawer; tap terminal toggle → bottom drawer
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Cmd+B toggle sidebar, Ctrl+` toggle terminal

performance_notes: Panels mount conditionally. State (open/closed) per panel preserved across viewport changes.
accessibility_across_viewports: Each pane is a landmark. Activity buttons have aria-pressed. Tab order: activity → side panel → editor → terminal.
edge_cases_handled: User resizes desktop to mobile mid-edit — editor stays, panels become drawers
User opens all panels on mobile — single drawer at a time
Terminal toggle persists across viewport changes
Keyboard shortcuts work in any layout
what_breaks_naive_approach: Cramming 4 panes on mobile = no usable editor space. Different IDEs per viewport = state loss.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Editor always primary. 2) Panels surface on demand. 3) Same shortcuts everywhere.

title: Frozen header AND footer in a scrollable table with virtual rows for performance
scenario_context: Table with 10,000 rows. Header stays at top, footer stays at bottom (totals row), middle scrolls. Virtual rendering for performance.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Position: fixed header (breaks when in narrow modal)
Sticky footer that scrolls with content
responsive_reasoning: 1. Sticky header + footer + virtualized middle.\n2. Works inside any scrollable container.\n3. Virtual library for performance.

detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Pure CSS for sticky, JS for virtualization
detection_strategy.code_pattern: position: sticky; top: 0; for header; bottom: 0; for footer

layout_strategy.content_priority: Header + footer always visible during scroll

performance_notes: Sticky pure CSS. Virtualize when >100 rows.
accessibility_across_viewports: Real <table> with thead/tbody/tfoot. tfoot has scope='row' on Total label.
edge_cases_handled: Short table (5 rows) — sticky still works
Very long table — virtualization keeps DOM small
Footer with horizontal scroll — sticky bottom + horizontal scroll combined work
Nested in narrow modal — same pattern works
what_breaks_naive_approach: Position: fixed assumes window-level, breaks in modals. Rendering all rows blocks main thread on 10k items.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Sticky works in any container. 2) Virtual rendering scales. 3) Same component for short and long tables.

title: Range slider with proper touch targets and keyboard accessibility
scenario_context: Price range slider in filters. Mobile users have fat fingers; desktop users have mouse precision. Native <input type='range'> is good but needs styling for 44pt thumb on mobile.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Tiny thumb on mobile (hard to grab)
Custom slider that loses keyboard support
responsive_reasoning: 1. Native input type=range is accessible by default.\n2. CSS pseudo-elements style the thumb to be 44pt on touch.\n3. Show current value clearly.

interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Arrow keys adjust, Page Up/Down for bigger steps

performance_notes: Native input — zero JS overhead. CSS handles thumb sizing.
accessibility_across_viewports: Native range is keyboard accessible by default. Live region announces current value. Min/max labels visible.
edge_cases_handled: Touch — larger thumb
Mouse — smaller, precise thumb
Keyboard — arrow keys work natively
Screen reader — announces current value via aria-live
what_breaks_naive_approach: Custom slider often loses keyboard support and a11y. Tiny thumb on mobile is hard to grab.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Native input — accessibility free. 2) Pointer-aware thumb size. 3) Live value announcement.

title: Animated GIF that pauses on prefers-reduced-motion and on mobile data saver
scenario_context: Marketing page uses animated GIFs. prefers-reduced-motion users need static images. Data saver users want minimal bandwidth.
responsive_reasoning: 1. Animated GIFs can trigger vestibular issues.\n2. Static poster is fallback for reduced-motion.\n3. Data preference also drives static.

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap static image to play animation explicitly

performance_notes: GIF only loaded when shouldAnimate true (browser cache reuses if toggled).
accessibility_across_viewports: Button with aria-label. Static fallback for reduced motion respects WCAG.
edge_cases_handled: User toggles OS reduced-motion — useReducedMotion fires, animation pauses
User on save-data — static only by default
User explicitly opts in via click — plays regardless
Screen reader — static alt text always available
what_breaks_naive_approach: Always-autoplaying GIFs are accessibility hostile. No alternative for motion-sensitive users.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Respects multiple preferences. 2) Opt-in for those who need motion. 3) Static fallback preserves content.

title: Sticky header that compresses (logo + nav) on scroll on desktop, hides on mobile
scenario_context: App header. Desktop: full header on top of page, compressed on scroll. Mobile: full header hides on scroll down (per responsive-029).
responsive_reasoning: 1. Desktop has room for compression — keeps brand visible.\n2. Mobile prioritizes content space — hide entirely.\n3. Same scroll signal, different transformations.

layout_strategy.content_priority: Logo always visible on desktop; content space on mobile
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Desktop compresses header height; mobile hides

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Scroll down → header hides; scroll up → reveals
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Scroll → header shrinks but stays visible

performance_notes: Scroll listener throttled to single state update. Transform GPU-accelerated.
accessibility_across_viewports: header is real landmark. Compression preserves all interactive elements. Hidden on mobile = transform-based, still in tab order.
edge_cases_handled: User scrolls down on mobile — hides
User scrolls up — reveals
User scrolls down on desktop — compresses
User at top of page — full size
Resize — picks correct behavior
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hiding desktop header annoys users who use it for navigation. Compressing mobile header eats precious vertical space.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Different behavior per viewport. 2) Compression keeps brand. 3) Hide maximizes mobile content space.

title: Popover that flips to drawer on mobile and respects collision detection on desktop
viewport_spectrum: Mobile (bottom drawer), desktop (anchored popover with collision)
scenario_context: Date picker popover. Desktop: anchored below trigger, flips up if no room below. Mobile: bottom drawer for thumb access.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Same popover on mobile cramped to a corner
responsive_reasoning: 1. Desktop: precision anchoring with collision detection.\n2. Mobile: bottom drawer is conventional.\n3. Same picker content.

interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Tab to field, Enter opens, Escape closes

performance_notes: Position calculated once per open. floating-ui recommended for production.
accessibility_across_viewports: role=dialog. Focus management needed in production (focus first interactive on open).
edge_cases_handled: Trigger at bottom of viewport — popover flips up
Trigger at right edge — popover clamps left
Resize during open — could trigger recalc
Mobile rotation — drawer adapts
what_breaks_naive_approach: Anchored popover off-screen is unusable. Same popover on mobile is hard to thumb-reach.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Drawer on mobile, popover on desktop. 2) Collision flip on desktop. 3) Same content.

title: WebView in iOS native apps: different from full Safari, narrower APIs available
viewport_spectrum: Web content embedded in native iOS apps (Instagram in-app browser, Twitter)
scenario_context: Web page rendered in iOS in-app webview (e.g., user opens link from Instagram). WebView lacks some APIs (no service worker on older iOS, different cookies). Need to detect and adapt.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Breaking when an API isn't available
responsive_reasoning: 1. WebView is restricted environment — gracefully degrade.\n2. Detect via UA (acceptable here since real Safari opens its own webview).\n3. Test for API availability before use.

detection_strategy.how_to_detect: UA string check (Instagram, FBAN, etc.) + feature detection

performance_notes: Single UA check on mount. Feature detection prevents API errors.
accessibility_across_viewports: Hint to open in browser is informational, not blocking.
edge_cases_handled: Instagram webview — banner shown, service worker skipped
Safari proper — full features
PWA standalone — different banner
Chrome on iOS — distinguished from Safari WebView
what_breaks_naive_approach: Assuming Safari features in webview crashes with feature-not-available errors. Forcing Safari open is hostile to user flow.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Environment detection. 2) Graceful degradation. 3) Informative without blocking.

title: Combined viewport + container query usage: a card that responds to both
viewport_spectrum: Card in various contexts on various viewports
scenario_context: A blog post card adapts to its container width (compact vs full). But also adapts subtle elements (font weight) to viewport via media queries (mobile gets slightly heavier weight for readability on low-DPI).
responsive_reasoning: 1. Container queries for layout (slot-aware).\n2. Media queries for global concerns (viewport-level prefs, DPR).\n3. Two scoping rules can coexist.

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Heavier font weight + tap target adjustments

performance_notes: Pure CSS combining both scopes.
accessibility_across_viewports: Heavier weight on mobile improves legibility on low-DPI screens (real concern for some Android devices).
edge_cases_handled: Card in narrow sidebar on desktop — container layout compact, viewport-level styles standard
Card full-width on mobile — both layout and typography adapt
Container 480+ on mobile (rare but possible) — both fire
what_breaks_naive_approach: Pure media queries miss container context. Pure container queries miss viewport concerns like DPR.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Two complementary query systems. 2) Container drives layout, viewport drives detail. 3) Composable scope.

title: Container query units (cqi, cqb, cqw, cqh) for sizing that responds to container
scenario_context: Card with content that should scale with the card itself: title font, image aspect, padding all scale relative to card width.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Using vw/vh (sizes to viewport, not container)
responsive_reasoning: 1. cqi (container query inline) = 1% of container's inline size.\n2. Use for text size, padding, gaps inside the card.\n3. With clamp for floor/ceiling.
performance_notes: Pure CSS. cqi computed once per container size change.
accessibility_across_viewports: Text remains within readable bounds via clamp floor.
edge_cases_handled: Card 200px wide — minimum sizes used
Card 800px wide — maximum sizes used
Card resized — all values adapt smoothly
Very small card (less than min) — clamp floor enforces minimum
what_breaks_naive_approach: vw units scale to viewport, not container. Hardcoded sizes don't adapt.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) cqi scales with container. 2) Clamp ensures readable bounds. 3) Truly modular component.

title: Subscript/superscript that scales proportionally without breaking line height
scenario_context: Scientific text with H2O, x², etc. Subscript/superscript should scale with parent text but not push line height.
responsive_reasoning: 1. Sub/sup are inline elements. Their size relative to text affects line-height.\n2. font-size: smaller + position: relative + vertical-align: baseline with line-height: 0.\n3. Sub/sup scale with parent (which is fluid via clamp).

detection_strategy.code_pattern: sub, sup { font-size: 0.75em; line-height: 0; position: relative; vertical-align: baseline; }

performance_notes: Pure CSS.
accessibility_across_viewports: Real sub/sup elements — screen readers announce them appropriately ('H sub 2 O' or similar).
edge_cases_handled: Multiple sub/sup in same line — line-height unaffected
Heading with sup (e.g., trademark™) — scales proportionally
Fluid heading with sup — sup scales too because em
what_breaks_naive_approach: Default browser sub/sup styling pushes line-height unexpectedly. Hardcoded px sizes don't scale with fluid type.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) em-based — scales with parent. 2) line-height: 0 prevents push. 3) Works at any fluid scale.

title: Card padding that scales with card size using cqi units
scenario_context: Cards in a feed should have proportional padding — small cards get tight padding, large cards get generous. cqi units handle this.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Fixed padding regardless of card size
responsive_reasoning: 1. Padding-to-card-width ratio should be visually consistent.\n2. cqi units scale with container.\n3. Combined with clamp for bounds.

layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Tighter on small, generous on large

performance_notes: Pure CSS, single layout pass.
accessibility_across_viewports: N/A for padding
edge_cases_handled: Tiny card 100px — minimum padding (0.5rem)
Large card 800px — maximum padding (1.5rem)
Resize — padding adapts
what_breaks_naive_approach: Fixed padding looks wrong at extremes. rem padding doesn't proportion.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) cqi-based proportional padding. 2) Clamp bounds. 3) Works at any card size.

title: Hover preview that becomes click-to-pin on touch (Twitter-style)
scenario_context: Tweet card shows preview on hover (desktop). On touch, tap to pin preview open until dismissed. Same preview content.
responsive_reasoning: 1. Hover-to-preview is desktop convention.\n2. Touch equivalent is tap-to-toggle.\n3. Same content, different trigger.

detection_strategy.code_pattern: @media (hover: hover) { .preview { opacity: 0; } .card:hover .preview { opacity: 1; } } @media (hover: none) { .preview { opacity: 0; } .card[data-pinned] .preview { opacity: 1; } }

layout_strategy.primary_technique: CSS hover for mouse, JS state for touch

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap to show preview, tap again or elsewhere to hide
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same as mobile or hover with trackpad
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Hover shows preview, mouse out hides

performance_notes: Listener attachment conditional on capability. State minimal.
accessibility_across_viewports: role differs by interaction (tooltip vs dialog) — appropriate. Close button on dialog. Focus-visible parity for keyboard.
edge_cases_handled: User on hybrid device — gets hover behavior
Touch user accidentally taps — preview pins, can dismiss with X
Keyboard user tabs to card — preview shows via focus
Multiple cards with previews — each independent
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hover-only locks out touch. Long-press conflicts with system. Click-only on desktop loses hover affordance.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Hover for mouse, tap-toggle for touch. 2) Focus parity for keyboard. 3) Same content.

title: Editable contenteditable div that scrolls with keyboard like a textarea would
scenario_context: Rich text editor with contenteditable. iOS doesn't auto-scroll contenteditable into view above keyboard like it does for input/textarea. Need manual handling.
responsive_reasoning: 1. contenteditable lacks native scroll-into-view on iOS.\n2. Manual scroll on selection change.\n3. visualViewport awareness for keyboard inset.

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap into editor, keyboard appears, cursor stays in view
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Cursor stays in view via native scroll
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Tab to enter, type, native scroll handles

performance_notes: selectionchange fires often; cheap check (contains + rect comparison).
accessibility_across_viewports: role=textbox aria-multiline. aria-label.
edge_cases_handled: User types at end of long doc — cursor scrolls into view
User taps middle of doc — cursor stays where placed (no unnecessary scroll)
Selection outside editor — early return
iOS rotation — visualViewport updates
what_breaks_naive_approach: Native contenteditable on iOS doesn't auto-scroll cursor above keyboard. Manual scroll fixes this.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) selectionchange listener. 2) visualViewport-aware threshold. 3) Smooth scroll preserves context.

title: Sticky sub-header within scrollable content that respects parent safe area
scenario_context: Article page has top header (safe area aware) and a sticky sub-header (section title) that stays below it. Sub-header must stick to bottom of main header, not viewport top.
responsive_reasoning: 1. Sticky offset must account for header height + safe area.\n2. CSS variable for header height.\n3. Calc combines variables.

detection_strategy.how_to_detect: CSS custom property set by JS or known constant

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Scroll content; sub-header sticks below header

performance_notes: Pure CSS sticky.
accessibility_across_viewports: Sub-header is real heading inside region. Sticky doesn't affect tab order.
edge_cases_handled: iPhone with notch — sub-header offset includes safe area
No safe area device — calc resolves correctly
Header height changes (collapse) — could use ResizeObserver in production
what_breaks_naive_approach: Sub-header at top: 0 overlaps main header. Hardcoded offset breaks on devices with safe area.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) CSS var + env() composable. 2) Multi-level sticky. 3) Safe area aware.

title: Recipe page that switches between vertical (mobile) and horizontal (landscape iPad) layout
scenario_context: Recipe with image + ingredients + steps. Mobile: image top, ingredients, then steps. Landscape iPad: image left, ingredients top-right, steps bottom-right.
responsive_reasoning: 1. Recipe is content-heavy; layout adapts to available space.\n2. Landscape gives horizontal real estate to use.\n3. Same content sections, different arrangement.

detection_strategy.code_pattern: grid-template-areas: 'image' 'ing' 'steps'; @media (orientation: landscape) and (min-width: 768px) { grid-template-areas: 'image ing' 'image steps'; }

performance_notes: Pure CSS grid areas.
accessibility_across_viewports: Logical DOM order preserved (image → ingredients → steps).
edge_cases_handled: Portrait phone — stacked
Landscape iPad — image fills left, content right
Desktop — same as landscape iPad
Long ingredients list — internal scroll
what_breaks_naive_approach: Stacked layout on landscape iPad wastes horizontal space.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Grid template areas for clarity. 2) Orientation-aware layout. 3) Same DOM, different visual arrangement.

title: iPad app with keyboard shortcuts that gracefully expose them when keyboard attached
scenario_context: App supports keyboard shortcuts (Cmd+S to save, etc.). On iPad with keyboard attached, show shortcut hints in tooltips/menus. Without keyboard, hide them (Cmd key doesn't exist).
responsive_reasoning: 1. Shortcuts visible only when input supports them.\n2. any-pointer:fine signals keyboard/trackpad.\n3. Modifier key adapts to platform.
performance_notes: Pure CSS + minimal JS.
accessibility_across_viewports: kbd is semantic. Hints don't affect screen reader content (they're additive).
edge_cases_handled: iPad without keyboard — no hints
iPad with Magic Keyboard — Mac-style hints
Windows laptop — Ctrl/Alt/Shift
Plain mobile — no hints
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hardcoded Ctrl+S on Mac shows wrong key. Always-shown hints clutter touch UIs.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Capability + platform aware. 2) Reactive to keyboard attachment. 3) Universal action with conditional hint.

title: Inbox triage view: list left, message right, swipe actions integrated
viewport_spectrum: Phone (one pane swipe), tablet (two pane), desktop (with preview)
scenario_context: Email inbox triage. Mobile: swipe actions on list items, single column. Tablet: master-detail. Desktop: master-detail + reading pane preview.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Swipe actions hidden on tablet+ (loses efficiency)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Same actions everywhere; presentation adapts.\n2. Mobile: swipe. Tablet+: hover/right-click + swipe too.\n3. Single source of truth for action handlers.

detection_strategy.code_pattern: Adaptive layout with shared item component

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Pane count adapts; item interactions consistent
layout_strategy.content_priority: Detail visible when selected; list always

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Swipe left to archive, right to delete; tap to read
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same gestures + tap to see detail
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Hover shows quick actions; click to read

performance_notes: List renders all items; virtualize for >100. Detail loads on selection.
accessibility_across_viewports: aria-current on selected item. Back button has aria-label on mobile. Heading hierarchy preserved.
edge_cases_handled: Mobile selects then goes back — list state preserved
Desktop rotation portrait — could switch to tablet layout
Empty inbox — empty state
Long subject — truncate with title attribute
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single-column on desktop wastes space. Three-column on mobile is cramped.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Same data, two presentations. 2) Mobile uses navigation; desktop uses split. 3) Shared item component.

title: Spreadsheet-style cell editing that respects keyboard on mobile and mouse on desktop
scenario_context: Inline-editable spreadsheet cells. Desktop: double-click to edit, Enter commits, Escape cancels. Mobile: tap to edit, virtual keyboard appears, done dismisses.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Tiny cells unreadable on mobile (need to pinch zoom)
Edit mode that requires multiple taps
responsive_reasoning: 1. Cell interaction model differs by input.\n2. Touch: single tap activates edit (one less tap than dbl-click).\n3. Mouse: dbl-click prevents accidental edit from cell selection.

detection_strategy.code_pattern: onDblClick={!isTouch ? edit : undefined} onClick={isTouch ? edit : select}

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap to edit, input gets focus + keyboard, tap elsewhere to commit
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Arrow keys navigate cells, Enter edits, Escape cancels

performance_notes: Input only mounts during edit. Single state per cell.
accessibility_across_viewports: role=button + tabIndex for keyboard. F2 keyboard shortcut (spreadsheet convention).
edge_cases_handled: Tap cell on iPad with keyboard — single tap edits (touch mode wins)
Mouse double-click — edits without accidental from selection
Keyboard Enter — edits
Tab during edit — commits and moves to next cell (production)
what_breaks_naive_approach: Double-click on touch needs second tap precision. Single click on desktop accidentally enters edit when user wanted to select.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Activation method per input. 2) F2/Enter for keyboard. 3) Native input UX during edit.

title: Phone number input with country picker that uses native picker on mobile
scenario_context: International phone number input. Mobile: native country picker. Desktop: dropdown with search. Number input always uses numeric keyboard on touch.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: JS country dropdown on mobile (worse than native)
No formatting feedback as user types
responsive_reasoning: 1. Country picker is select-with-many-options; mobile native is better.\n2. Desktop benefits from searchable combobox.\n3. Number input uses inputMode='tel'.

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap country flag → native picker; type digits with numeric keyboard
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Tab to flag, Enter opens, type to filter, type number

performance_notes: Native select on mobile = zero JS. Number type triggers tel keyboard.
accessibility_across_viewports: Country and number both labeled. autoComplete='tel-national' enables autofill.
edge_cases_handled: iOS — native country picker with searchable list
Android — native scrollable picker
Desktop — combobox with search
Autofill — country and number filled appropriately
what_breaks_naive_approach: Custom dropdown on iOS for 200 countries is laggy and harder than native. autoComplete='off' kills autofill.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Native on mobile, combobox on desktop. 2) tel inputMode triggers correct keyboard. 3) Autocomplete tokens.

title: Iframe embed that maintains aspect ratio across viewports
viewport_spectrum: Various with iframe content (YouTube, Vimeo, maps)
scenario_context: YouTube video embed. Should maintain 16:9 aspect ratio at any width without iframe attributes. CSS aspect-ratio + parent container.
responsive_reasoning: 1. CSS aspect-ratio is native solution.\n2. iframe fills container.\n3. lazy attribute defers loading.
performance_notes: lazy attribute defers iframe load. aspect-ratio reserves space immediately (no CLS).
accessibility_across_viewports: iframe title attribute is required for screen readers. allowFullScreen enables fullscreen via keyboard.
edge_cases_handled: 9:16 vertical video — aspect=9/16
1:1 square — aspect=1/1
Browser without aspect-ratio (very old) — could fallback to padding-bottom hack via @supports
iframe blocked (cookies, etc.) — empty container with reserved space, doesn't shift layout
what_breaks_naive_approach: Fixed iframe dimensions break responsive. No title fails WCAG. No lazy loading wastes data on initial page load.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Native CSS aspect-ratio. 2) Lazy loaded. 3) Accessible via title.

title: Sub-navigation rail that converts to horizontal scroll pills on mobile
viewport_spectrum: Desktop sub-nav rail, mobile scrolling pills
scenario_context: Settings page has sub-navigation: General, Account, Notifications, Security, Privacy, Billing. Desktop: vertical rail. Mobile: horizontal scrolling pills above content.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Vertical rail on mobile (eats too much horizontal space)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Vertical rail works on desktop with horizontal real estate.\n2. Horizontal pill scroll is mobile-friendly tab-like pattern.\n3. Same nav items, two presentations.

layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Pills are compact; rail more readable

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Swipe pills horizontally; tap to navigate
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same or rail depending on width

performance_notes: Single React Link per item. Native scroll on mobile pills.
accessibility_across_viewports: nav landmark with aria-label. aria-current=page on active. Real links work with browser back button. Pills have whitespace-nowrap so they don't break.
edge_cases_handled: Many sections — pills scroll horizontally
Long section names — pills don't wrap awkwardly
Active item far right — could auto-scroll into view (production)
Resize from desktop to mobile — current section preserved
what_breaks_naive_approach: Vertical rail on mobile eats 30% of horizontal space. Hamburger sub-nav adds extra tap.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Pills vs rail per viewport. 2) Same data. 3) Touch-friendly horizontal scroll.
